Massachusetts State Spending Audit · Fiscal Year 2025

Source: Comptroller CTHRU (2,864,979 transactions, 165,851 payroll records) × COMMBUYS (17,275 contracts). Generated 2026-04-05. Click any row marked ▸ to expand.

Summary By Object Class By Cabinet Top Vendors Payroll Overtime Competition Analysis Attention & Waste Trusts Methodology

Executive Summary

Total FY2025 outlays were $98.3B across 2,864,979 transactions, 31,865 vendors, 160 departments and 39 cabinets. About 17% falls in classes that are, in principle, biddable. The remainder is statutory — benefits, local aid, pensions, payroll, and debt service.

Spend by Object Class (click to drill down)

Every line of state spending is tagged with an object class. Click any class for its top vendors, top departments, and an explanation of what that category actually contains.

How to read this: The top three classes (RR Benefits, PP State Aid, DD Pensions) are statutory / formula obligations — they are not procured. The procurable universe (MM, NN, HH, UU, FF, GG, JJ, KK, LL, EE) is roughly 17% of total spend.

Payroll Analysis (CTHRU Open Payroll)

Individual-level payroll data for all state employees, sourced from the Comptroller's CTHRU open payroll dataset (2.69M records, CY2010-2025). FY2025: 165,851 employees, $10.89B total compensation.

Public context: Massachusetts publishes individual state employee compensation through CTHRU per M.G.L. Chapter 7 §4A. This includes executive branch, judiciary, legislature, UMass system, and constitutional offices. Quasi-public agencies (MassDOT/MBTA, MassPort) are included since CY2018.

Key trend: Total overtime statewide grew from $553M (2023) → $617M (2024) → $651M (2025), an 18% increase over two years, significantly outpacing headcount and base pay growth.

Overtime Deep Dive $651M

Overtime is the single largest discretionary payroll lever and a perennial audit concern. $651M in FY2025 represents 6.0% of total pay — concentrated in corrections, state police, MBTA, and mental health facilities.

Why this matters: Massachusetts OIG has repeatedly flagged overtime abuse in corrections and state police. The Suffolk County Sheriff's office (SDS) has an OT-to-pay ratio of 32.4% — nearly 1 in 3 payroll dollars is overtime. MBTA overtime ($133.6M) has been the subject of multiple FMCB/DPU audits since 2019. State Police OT was at the center of the Troop E overtime scandal (2017-2018 federal prosecutions).

Individual employees earning 2-3x their annual rate are statistically anomalous and warrant review — these are flagged in the table below.

Overtime by Department (3-year trend, click to expand) review

DepartmentFY23 OTFY24 OT FY25 OT2yr GrowthOT/Total

Employees Earning 2x+ Their Annual Rate anomaly

These individuals' total compensation exceeds double their annual salary rate — the excess comes from overtime, buyouts, or other pay. Common in 24/7 facilities (corrections, hospitals) but the extreme cases warrant review.

NameDeptTitleAnnual Rate Actual PayOvertimeRatio

Procurement Competition Analysis

Cross-reference with scraped COMMBUYS contract database. Shows how much FY2025 spend actually flowed through an openly-competed bid.

Key finding: Only about 11% of total FY2025 spend touches a COMMBUYS contract with an OPEN competitive bid on file. The rest is either statutory (benefits, aid, pensions, debt, payroll — not procurable) or is purchased under closed/expired/no-bid contracts.

Attention & Potential Waste

Curated red flags drawn from the data. None of these are proof of waste — they are statistically unusual patterns that justify a closer look.

Methodology note: These flags use well-understood audit heuristics (single-source contracts, round-dollar amounts, rapid YoY growth, concentration in HH/UU classes). Public context where applicable:
  • Boston Medical Center receives MassHealth DSH and safety-net supplemental payments outside of open bidding — rate-set by EOHHS, reviewed by CHIA.
  • Chapter 70 / Chapter 90 school and road aid is formula-based (G.L. c.70, c.90).
  • MassHealth MCOs (BMC HealthNet, Tufts TogetherHealth, WellSense) are capitated at actuarially-set PMPM rates; procurement is via periodic 5-year MCO re-procurement.
  • IT firms (Accenture, Deloitte, IBM, Tyler Technologies, Salesforce resellers) are the most frequent targets of OIG and Inspector General reviews for rate-card abuse and sole-source renewals.

IT & Consulting Concentration (Object Classes HH + UU) focus

VendorAmountDeptsTxns

Round-dollar transactions ≥ $1M (multiples of $100k) flag

Transactions that are exact multiples of $100,000 above $1M are statistically rare in invoice-driven spending. They often correspond to grants, transfers, or lump-sum advances — legitimate in most cases, but worth spot-checking.

VendorDepartmentObject ClassDateAmount

Trust-Fund Spending (3TN)

Trust funds operate partially outside normal appropriation controls. They are a recurring topic in the State Auditor's reports.

VendorAmountTxns

Methodology & Limitations

  • Spending data: Comptroller CTHRU open data, 47.6M transactions 2010-2026. FY2025 = 2.86M transactions, 31,865 distinct vendors, 160 departments.
  • Payroll data: CTHRU Open Payroll (Socrata dataset 9ttk-7vz6), 2.69M records covering CY2010-2025. Includes individual name, department, position, base pay, overtime, buyout, annual rate, bargaining group. Updated bi-weekly.
  • Contract data: 17,275 active + historical contract blankets scraped from COMMBUYS (www.commbuys.com), with 17,270 Purchase Order detail pages and 1,693 Bid detail pages fetched and parsed into structured fields.
  • Vendor matching normalizes company names (strip LLC/INC/CORP, punctuation, case) and joins spending ↔ contracts on the normalized key. This is imperfect — DBA names, mergers, and typos cause false negatives.
  • "Open bid" = a COMMBUYS bid_type containing "OPEN". "Closed" bids are historical. "No bid" = contract has no bid reference (typically legacy conversions, rate-set providers, or inter-agency agreements).
  • Limitations: MassDOT and DCAMM capital projects have separate procurement systems; they are visible in CTHRU (NN class) but not in COMMBUYS. MassHealth MCO payments do not go through COMMBUYS at all.
  • Nothing here alleges misconduct. Flags are patterns, not findings.
Generated locally from spending.db + commbuys.db. No data leaves your machine.
